title: Felting needle safety and selection
subtitle: How barbed needles work, how to choose the right gauge, and how to work safely
excerpt: Felting needles are barbed instruments that can cause serious puncture wounds if used incorrectly. This short reading explains how the needles work, how to choose the right gauge for the job, and the simple safety disciplines that make needle felting an activity with a very low accident rate.
sourceNotes: Felting needle design and gauge specifications are documented in the industrial non-woven textile industry. Gauge numbers and barb geometry are standard specifications; the safety guidance is synthesised from the physical properties of barbed needles and the mechanics of puncture wounds.

content.text: A felting needle is a steel rod with a series of tiny barbed notches cut into the lower shaft. When the needle enters roving, the barbs catch individual wool fibres and drag them downward, entangling them with fibres beneath. Withdraw the needle and the barbs release. Repeat a few thousand times and the fibres are locked together into felt. The mechanism is purely mechanical: no heat, no adhesive, no chemistry.

attrs.body: Wash any needle puncture with soap and water. Press until bleeding stops. Watch the site for two days.
attrs.title: Wound risk

content.text: together determine what a needle is good for.

Size 36 (coarse) is the aggressive shaping needle. It moves the most fibre per stroke and builds density fastest. Use it for the initial compaction of a large base shape (an egg-shaped body, a ball, a thick panel). It leaves visible puncture marks on the surface, so it is a rough-working needle rather than a finishing needle. Some people skip size 36 entirely and start all work with size 38.

Size 38 (medium) is the workhorse. It builds density reasonably fast, leaves a less marked surface than size 36, and works well for both shaping and colour work. If you own only one size of needle, own size 38.

Size 40 (fine) is the surface and detail needle. It works shallowly and lightly, smoothing the surface and compacting colour patches without disturbing the layers beneath. Use it for eye detail, surface blending, and the final smoothing pass on any needle-felted piece. Stab it only 3-5 mm deep; full-depth stabs with a fine needle can bend or break the shaft.

Keep the foam pad large enough that there is always foam directly below the point where the needle will exit the work. The needle must stab into foam, not onto a hard surface: on a hard surface the needle either breaks or bounces back up.

Stab straight in and straight out at 90 degrees to the work surface. The barbs are designed to slide in and out along the axis of the needle shaft. An angled stab means the barbs enter at an angle and exit at the same angle, catching on the internal structure of the felt and snapping the needle shaft.

For flat panels, lay the supporting hand flat beside the work, not beneath it or curled around it. For three-dimensional forms, lay the form on the foam mat and rotate it rather than holding it in the fist and needling the sides.

Broken needle tips: when a needle breaks, find the broken piece before continuing. A barbed needle fragment in a felt piece is difficult to locate by touch alone. Sweep a small neodymium magnet across the work surface and the foam pad to locate any steel fragment before it disappears.

Store felting needles point-down in a small jar with a few centimetres of rice or coarse sand at the bottom to hold them upright. Never leave loose needles on a work surface: they are nearly invisible against most textures and are easily knocked to the floor. Felting needles in carpet are a household hazard.
